📋 How to Follow — T+1 Execution Guide
Weekly signal Every Friday close — rank NQ100 stocks by 11-1 weekly momentum (44-week return minus 4-week skip). Identify top-3. Execute rebalance at Monday open (T+1). Only trade positions that changed. Daily regime watch Each day check: Is NQ100 EW index > 200d MA? Is SPY > 200d MA? If regime flips to BEAR mid-month, exit active sleeve to cash at next open (T+1) — don't wait for month end. Bear re-entry When regime returns BULL, wait for next month-end signal to pick fresh top-3. Don't chase mid-month re-entries — false positives cost more than the lag. Breadth check Confirm ≥40% of NQ100 stocks have positive 11-1 score before deploying active sleeve. Below threshold = hold cash even if MA regime says BULL. Position sizes Bull: 35% SPY + 21.7% each of top-3 stocks. Bear: 10% SPY + 90% cash/money-market. Rebalance tolerances: ±2% before trading. Stop-loss If any holding drops >10% from your entry price, sell at next open (T+1) and replace with the next-best ranked NQ100 stock not already held. Check prices daily.
⚠️ Backtest uses 2026 NQ100 universe — survivorship bias inflates CAGR ~4–6pts. Past results are not a guarantee of future performance. This is not financial advice.
Notes & Follow-Up
Performance Summary
Strategy Mechanics
Optimised allocation (20Y backtest): Dynamic SPY + Active based on dual regime signal.
  • Bull regime: 35% SPY + 65% active (21.7% each in top-3 NQ100 11-1 momentum stocks)
  • Bear regime: 10% SPY + 90% cash — near-full exit protects against deep drawdowns
Dual regime trigger (both must be true for Bull):
  ① NQ100 equal-weight > its 200d MA   ② SPY > its own 200d MA   ③ Breadth ≥ 40% (≥40% of NQ100 stocks have positive 11-1 score)
Momentum: 11-1 cross-sectional (11-month return minus 1-month skip, ranked within NQ100 universe)
⚠️ Survivorship Bias Warning: This backtest uses the current 2026 NQ100 composition applied to historical data. The real 2006 NQ100 included Yahoo, BlackBerry (RIMM), Palm, and Sun Microsystems — many of which failed. CAGR is likely overstated by ~4–6 percentage points. Treat absolute return numbers as an upper bound; risk management conclusions (MaxDD, regime behaviour) are more reliable.
Key insight: The fixed 60% SPY floor was the MaxDD bottleneck — SPY itself lost 51% in 2008. Making both sleeves regime-sensitive and adding breadth confirmation cuts MaxDD from −38% → −17% while raising CAGR from 21% → 30%.
System E — 20Y backtest net of costs (Jan 2006 – Jun 2026) | 4-fold OOS validated

GFC 2008 Regime Lag

Monthly trigger

116d lag
⚡ Is Monthly Too Slow? — Regime Detection Lag Analysis
Key question: When a bear market starts, how many days does the monthly system take to exit vs a daily-monitored system?

The regime signal (NQ100 EW + SPY vs their 200d MAs) is computed on daily prices. A daily system exits T+1 when it first trips bear. A monthly system waits until end-of-month. This lag can be costly in fast-moving crashes.
Bear Market Daily Trigger Monthly Trigger Lag SPY Peak→Trough
GFC 2008-09 2007-08-06 2007-11-30 116d lag -55.2%
COVID 2020 2020-02-28 2020-02-29 1d lag -33.7%
Bear 2022 2022-01-24 2022-02-28 35d lag -24.5%
⚠️ GFC 2008 — 116 day lag
Daily regime tripped Aug 2007 as NQ100 proxy stocks started rolling over. Monthly didn't exit until Nov 2007. However — the worst of GFC was Sep–Nov 2008. Both systems exited well before then. Monthly system still achieves -17% MaxDD in GFC.
✓ COVID 2020 — 1 day lag
COVID was so fast (full -34% SPY in 5 weeks) that daily and monthly systems both triggered on virtually the same day — Feb 28/29. For V-shaped crashes, monthly frequency is essentially as responsive as daily.
⚡ Bear 2022 — 35 day lag
Daily triggered Jan 24, monthly Feb 28. The 35-day lag cost some drawdown in 2022's slow-burn decline, but the strategy's dual regime + breadth filter still limited losses well vs SPY's -24.5% drop.
Conclusion: Monthly is NOT fundamentally too slow — the dual-regime filter (NQ100+SPY both vs 200d MA) provides sufficient early warning in all three major bear markets. The real improvement comes from weekly momentum rebalancing (faster rotation into stronger stocks) and per-position stop-loss (cuts catastrophic single-stock losses mid-period). A practical hybrid: keep monthly regime checks, add a daily portfolio DD circuit breaker (-8% from month-start → exit active sleeve next day).
📊 System Comparison — Equity Curves
* Systems C and E use weekly momentum approximated from monthly data (limited daily NQ100 prices available). CAGR directionally correct but likely overstated 3–6pts due to data approximation.
📋 System Performance Comparison
System CAGR MaxDD Calmar Sharpe Vol
■ A: Monthly (baseline) 28.31% -17.03% 1.662 1.342 20.75%
■ B: Daily regime 19.61% -21.91% 0.895 1.141 17.05%
■ C: Weekly momentum* *approx (limited daily data) 39.16% -17.45% 2.243 1.575 22.78%
■ D: Monthly + stop-loss 16.45% -22.65% 0.726 1.044 15.88%
■ E: Weekly + stop* *approx (limited daily data) 46.29% -13.21% 4.254 1.867 21.87%
■ SPY 10.92% -50.78% 0.215 0.765 15.34%
🎯 Practical Recommendations — Upgrading the System
✓ Keep (already optimal)
✓ Monthly momentum signal (11-1 cross-sectional)
✓ Dual regime: NQ100 EW + SPY vs 200d MA
✓ Breadth filter ≥40%
✓ Dynamic allocation (35%/10% SPY, 65%/0% active)
✓ Rebalance at month-end, execute T+1 open
⚡ Add for improved performance
⚡ Daily portfolio circuit breaker: if portfolio drops >8% from month-start NAV, exit active sleeve to cash T+1 (re-enter at next month-end signal)
⚡ Per-position stop-loss: if any single holding drops >12% from entry price, close that position T+1 and allocate to next-best stock (or cash if no qualifying substitute)
⚡ Weekly re-rank (future): if you can source weekly price data for all NQ100 stocks, refreshing momentum rankings weekly (keeping same 11-1 lookback on weekly bars) should meaningfully improve rotation speed. Tests suggest +10–18% CAGR vs monthly, but requires reliable weekly data source.
⚠️ Data limitation note: System B (pure daily regime) underperforms monthly in this test because daily NQ100 stock prices are only available for ~14 key tickers. For the remaining 85 NQ100 stocks, SPY returns were used as a proxy — this understates active sleeve performance and makes daily whipsaw look worse than it truly is. A properly implemented daily system with full daily price data would likely outperform. The weekly approximation (C/E) is similarly limited.
Alpha Trading Desk · System E: Weekly 11-1 + Daily Regime T+1 + Stop −10% · 20Y net: 38.4% CAGR / -21.59% MaxDD / Calmar 1.778 · 2026-06-29
Key finding: Both methods converge at Lookback=11, Skip=1 — within the NQ100 universe (all high-beta tech), cross-sectional ranking and RS-vs-SPY ranking produce nearly identical top-3 selections. The stocks that rose most within NQ100 are also the ones that beat SPY by the widest margin, so the methods agree.
Optimal: CS 11-1 → Sharpe 1.243 vs current 12-1 Sharpe 1.167 (+0.076). Why 11 months? Slightly shorter lookback captures more recent momentum shifts while still filtering out noise, without the 24-month lag that includes stale trends.

Breadth filter insight: Market breadth = % of NQ100 stocks with positive 12-1 momentum score. When breadth is low (few stocks leading), momentum becomes fragile — those few leaders can reverse sharply. Adding a breadth gate (e.g. ≥30%) means the active sleeve only deploys when the rally is broad-based. Combined with a 50% active sleeve (vs current 40%), this delivers Calmar 0.766 vs current 0.55 — 25.5% CAGR and only -33% MaxDD over 20 years.
Peak-entry test: If you started at the very worst month (peak before a crash), the worst 36-month drawdown for the best Calmar config was -33.3% vs current strategy's -38.6% and SPY's -50.8%.

Answer: Yes — MaxDD < 20% is achievable with HIGHER CAGR than current.
The fix: apply a separate 200d MA filter to the SPY sleeve (not just the active sleeve). When SPY < its own 200d MA, the SPY allocation drops from 35% → 10% (minimal). This alone cuts MaxDD from −38% → −17%. Pair that with 65% active sleeve in bull + breadth≥40% filter and you get: 29.93% CAGR, −17.03% MaxDD, Calmar 1.757 over 20 years.
Why it works in 2008: SPY broke its 200d MA in Oct 2007 — 6 months before the worst crash. The strategy went near-cash on both sleeves, missing most of the −51% SPY drop.
TLT (bond rotation) available — tested as bear-market alternative to cash.
